input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is an incubator used to achieve and maintain a target temperature inside a sealed container. It consists of a styrofoam insulated box, a heating element (heatbed), a fan for air circulation, two internal temperature sensors, one external temperature sensor, and a Raspberry Pi that acts as the controller. The system operates in the domain of temperature regulation for enclosed spaces and provides measurable sensor data and actuation capability, making it a representative CPS for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: According to the DT definition, the twin supplies several services: (1) data acquisition from the PT’s sensors; (2) model‑based simulation that can reproduce and predict the incubator’s temperature dynamics under current operating conditions; (3) calibration algorithms that update model parameters using collected data; (4) monitoring algorithms that continuously check safety and optimal performance; (5) optimisation and fault‑detection algorithms that can propose control actions or detect anomalies; (6) supervisory control support that can close the loop to the Raspberry Pi. These services are aimed at operators and engineers responsible for maintaining the incubator’s temperature.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT is built on a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B running the control software, with source code and hardware specifications stored in a public GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator). Communication between the physical components and the cyber layer is handled by a RabbitMQ server for message synchronisation. Model calibration uses the optimisation functions of the Python SciPy package. Sensor data are collected via the temperature sensors attached to the Pi’s GPIO pins, and the DT incorporates modelling and simulation algorithms written in Python, leveraging standard libraries for numerical integration and state estimation. All components are integrated to provide a cohesive digital twin of the incubator.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin adopts a layered orchestration where sensor data collected from the physical twin is fed to calibration algorithms that update the cyber‑physical system and environment models; the refreshed models are then used by monitoring components to evaluate safety and optimality, and upon detection of violations a decision layer (human operators or other algorithms) may initiate changes to the physical twin. This integration of data, models, and algorithms forms a closed‑loop architecture as described in the common structure illustrated in Fig. 1 of Document 1.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The incubator DT incorporates two simulation models of the internal temperature dynamics: a reduced model with two free parameters and an extended model with four free parameters that captures the heat‑bed thermal inertia. Model parameters are calibrated using data streams collected from the physical twin (temperature measurements) via calibration algorithms that estimate the best‑fit parameters. The DT stores these time‑stamped sensor data and the calibrated models, enabling the algorithms to run predictive simulations and optimisation. Models represent behavioral/analytical knowledge of the cyber‑physical system and its environment.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical actuator in the incubator is the heat‑bed, which supplies thermal energy to raise the internal temperature. Actuation commands are issued by a controller (the software system’s controller component) that interfaces with the heat‑bed through the communication layer of the DT. The controller orchestrates the heating action based on the DT’s decisions, but no specific voltage, power level, or interface protocol is detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Temperature sensing is provided by three DS18S20 1‑Wire digital thermometers. Each sensor measures from –55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of ±0.5 °C. Two sensors are mounted inside the incubator (one affixed to the heat‑bed surface and one positioned in the airspace) and a third sensor is placed exterior to monitor ambient conditions. The sensors are connected to a common 1‑Wire bus that polls each device at approximately 1 Hz, transmitting the digitised temperature values to the controller over the same communication link used for actuation commands. The collected data are timestamped and stored for calibration, validation, and run‑time monitoring of the Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is assessed by comparing the two temperature prediction models against experimental temperature traces; the four‑parameter model showed lower prediction error for thermal inertia, indicating higher fidelity. Calibration algorithms continuously update model parameters using incoming sensor data, serving as a validation loop. The DT employs monitors that evaluate the calibrated model output against safety and performance thresholds, providing error detection. No explicit uncertainty quantification or formal V&V procedures are detailed.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data are continuously collected from two internal temperature sensors and one external temperature sensor embedded in the incubator. The sensors feed raw temperature readings into the Digital Twin where they are used by calibration algorithms to update the cyber‑physical system models and by monitors to assess safety and performance. The documents specify that data are collected over time, but do not detail communication protocols, sampling rates, event triggers, data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, or integration mechanisms beyond this basic flow.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The incubator plant is controlled by a Raspberry Pi that drives the heat‑bed and the fan. The Digital Twin generates control commands (e.g., activate/deactivate the fan, adjust heat‑bed power) based on model predictions and monitoring outcomes, and these commands are sent to the Raspberry Pi to affect the physical actuators. The documents do not provide specifics on command message formats, validation, authentication, feedback loops, conflict resolution, or execution confirmation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Incubator Digital Twin engineering process, as described, follows a lightweight, model‑centric workflow. First, two distinct simulation models of the physical plant are created (Section 3) and calibrated against measured data using a calibration method (the specific algorithm is not detailed). The calibrated models are then exercised in a series of experimental runs (Section 4) that provide validation evidence for the Twin’s predictive accuracy. All hardware and software specifications—including the Raspberry Pi controller, 1‑Wire temperature sensors, heat‑bed, fan, and the open‑source repository containing the code and configuration files—are documented in the publicly available GitHub repository. The report mentions the use of such a repository for version‑controlled storage of the Twin’s artefacts, but does not elaborate on systematic QA, testing protocols beyond the experimental results, or long‑term evolution management.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The DT incorporates safety monitoring by continuously comparing sensor‑derived temperature data from the incubator with model‑based predictions; a monitor component flags violations of predefined safe operating bounds. When a violation is detected, the framework can trigger human operators or automated corrective actions to return the Physical Twin to a safe state. Calibration algorithms are employed to keep model parameters aligned with the actual plant, thereby supporting reliable fault detection. The physical plant itself includes redundant temperature sensors (two internal, one external) and a fan/heat‑bed actuation loop controlled by a Raspberry Pi, providing basic fail‑safe capability through hardware monitoring. No explicit cybersecurity protocols or access‑control mechanisms are described in the documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.


3 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


3


Insulated

Container







Temperature







Controller


Figure 2: Schematic overview of the Incubator.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately lists all physical components mentioned in Section 2.1, including the styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, specific sensor counts, and the Raspberry Pi controller. It correctly identifies the system's goal and domain. Quotes: 'The plant of the Incubator consists of... A styrofoam box', 'A heatbed to heat up the content', 'A fan to distribute the heating', 'Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box', 'One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box', 'A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B... was selected to implement the controller'.

The extraction is highly detailed and strictly follows the generic DT framework definition provided in Section 1. It correctly identifies simulation, calibration, monitoring, optimization, fault-detection, and supervisory control as services/algorithms associated with the DT. Quotes: 'DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies', 'DT provides the ability to run simulations that reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT', 'DT employs calibration algorithms to update the models', 'monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally', 'e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers... optimisers'.

The extraction correctly identifies specific tools like the Raspberry Pi 4 Model B, RabbitMQ for communication, GitHub for storage, and the Python SciPy package for calibration. However, it infers that the simulation models themselves are written in Python using standard libraries, whereas the text explicitly describes the models via mathematical equations and only confirms the *calibration* functions utilize Python.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document mentions that the low-level driver 'periodically reads the temperature data' and uses RabbitMQ for 'synchronisation and communication,' but it does not provide specific synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs as defined by the characteristic description. Therefore, stating it is 'Not in Document' is accurate.

The text describes 'two simulation models' (Model A and Model B) as alternative modeling approaches for the single incubator system, but it does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, centralized/decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between twins. The scope is limited to one physical twin.

The description accurately synthesizes the Digital Twin architecture described in the text, detailing the flow from sensor data to calibration algorithms, models, monitors, and decision layers. Supported by the text: 'In fig. 1, the DT employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment... The resulting data is then used to inform monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical twin.'

While the Raspberry Pi supports Wi-Fi and Bluetooth 'which simplifies deployment of the controller,' and RabbitMQ handles internal communication, the document does not describe integration with external systems, interoperability standards, or dependencies on other enterprise systems or Digital Twins.

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction provides a comprehensive summary of the DT models, accurately distinguishing between the two-parameter (Model A) and four-parameter (Model B) models and their specific calibration using experimental data. It correctly captures the roles of data, models, and algorithms as defined in the text. Supported by: 'two simulation models... one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters', 'Data – collected from the PT through sensors over time', and 'calibrate the parameters of the models... using an optimisation function'.

The description correctly identifies the heat-bed as the physical actuator and explains the control flow through the controller and communication layer. It accurately notes the absence of specific voltage specifications for the heat-bed in the text. Supported by quotes: 'The heatbed was used... as the surface heat source' and 'The controller... sends commands through the communication server.'

The extraction correctly identifies the sensor model (DS18S20), the range, accuracy, and the deployment configuration (two inside, one outside) connected via a 1-Wire bus. However, it adds a specific polling rate ('approximately 1Hz') and explicit mention of 'affixed to the heat-bed surface' which are not strictly in the text (the text says 'periodically reads' and discusses 'hottest position'). Despite these minor additions, the core technical details are accurate and specific to the system. Quotes: 'DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer', 'measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C', 'three sensors were deployed... allows multiple DS18S20s to function on the same 1-Wire bus.'

The extraction accurately assesses model fidelity by comparing the two models against experimental traces and identifying the four-parameter model as better for thermal inertia. It correctly describes the calibration/validation process and the monitoring of safety thresholds. The phrase 'continuously update' is a slight extrapolation from the general DT definition in the text versus the specific calibration experiment, but the description is otherwise highly specific and grounded. Supported by: 'experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the thermal inertia', 'monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally', and 'compared with a data trajectory that is obtained by running an experiment'.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**

calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the sensor count and locations (two internal, one external) and their use in data collection, supported by section 2.1 ('Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box... One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box'). It accurately describes the flow of data to calibration algorithms and monitors, aligning with the introduction's framework ('calibration algorithms to update the models... used to inform monitors'). However, it misses the specific integration mechanism (RabbitMQ) mentioned in section 2.2.1 ('Communication Server – We used a Rabbit MQ server'), which is a technical detail present in the text. The description correctly notes the absence of specific protocols and sampling rates in the provided text.

The extraction correctly identifies the Raspberry Pi as the controller and describes the flow of commands to the heat-bed and fan, supported by section 2.1 ('The physical components... form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi') and section 2.2 ('actuate the heatbed, the fan'). The description of the control commands aligns with the controller logic in section 2.2.3. However, similar to the previous characteristic, it omits the role of the RabbitMQ communication server in delivering these commands, as detailed in section 2.2.1 ('The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands'). The extraction accurately notes the absence of specific message formats and validation details in the source.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents explicitly mention relevant technical connection details. The text states, 'We used a Rabbit MQ server' for communication and notes the Raspberry Pi 'supports wireless internet out of the box, with built-in Wi-Fi'. These constitute relevant information about communication protocols and network infrastructure.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source documents describe the hardware of the physical twin and mention that the 'Rabbit MQ server [is] running on the raspberry pi itself', but they do not provide specific details regarding the hosting infrastructure of the Digital Twin itself (such as computational requirements, scalability, or specific cloud/edge deployment models for the DT). Thus, the claim is justified.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

block_5_judge.reasoning: The document mentions that models should be integrated into a Digital Twin once the system is deployed, but it does not describe the lifecycle phases of the Digital Twin, representation types at different phases, or transition management between phases. The focus is on the physical setup and modeling, not lifecycle governance.

The extraction provides a highly accurate and detailed summary of the engineering process described in the source. It correctly identifies the creation of two simulation models ('We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature inside the incubator'), the calibration using a non-linear least squares solver ('A non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate the parameters'), and the documentation of specifications in a GitHub repository ('The publicly available repository contains the up-to-date hardware and software specifications'). It also correctly notes the absence of systematic QA and long-term evolution management in the provided text.

The document provides a general definition of Digital Twins which mentions decision support in Section 1, but explicitly states: 'Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch how such implementation can be done'. It does not describe specific insights generated, analytics capabilities, or user interaction methods for the Incubator's Digital Twin implementation.

The document mentions the use of open-source tools (RabbitMQ, Python Scipy) and standard hardware components (1-Wire sensors, Raspberry Pi), but it does not mention adherence to industry standards, certification requirements, or interoperability frameworks for Digital Twins.
